note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence pauses (never rewritten, summarized, or reordered; stage directions and pronunciation spellings kept exactly as written in the script, e.g. 'Customer three-sixty', 'Open Edge, Smart Tech, Other, and Card Connect'). Block windows are MEASURED onsets, not the nominal formula: Sigma(ffprobe per-file audio + 0.4) puts the s13 end at 245.134s, which overshoots the ffprobe-probed master (244.689s) by 0.445s (> 0.3 tolerance) because the concatenated MP3 frames decode ~0.03-0.46s shorter than ffprobe's per-file duration sum. Onsets were therefore measured by normalized cross-correlation of each WC-PB-04-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (all 13 blocks matched at score 1.000). Master ffprobe duration 244.689s.

phrases.text: Start on the Command Center.
Down the left column, one number sizes the whole problem: the decline rate.
Right now it reads a quarter of a percent, and the trend arrow points down.
That's the entire network, not one member.
Read it before you chase anyone.
One expired card won't move it. A gateway going bad will.
Now scroll to Priority Intelligence.
WashCentral is already working the problem.
One play flags eighteen recoverable billing failures through the card updater,
worth about six hundred forty dollars to recover.
Another shows the retry automation churning: thirty-seven queued, eleven already recovered.
You're reading these, not launching them.
Before you click anything, size it.
One member says their card bounced? Head to Customer three-sixty.
A wave of failures at one site? The declined card log, then that site's gateway.
Charges about texts, not cards? That's a billing question.
Same ticket, three different trails.
Take the single-customer trail first. Open the member from the customer list.
Customer three-sixty opens on nine tabs, everything about this account in one place.
The billing answers live two tabs in.
Open the Membership tab.
Find the billing cycle block. Two fields answer the ticket.
Last payment result tells you whether the most recent charge failed.
Next charge tells you when the system tries again.
That's the member's side of the story in ten seconds.
Now the Payments tab.
Here's the card on file and its expiry.
Most single-member declines are just that: a card that expired or got pulled.
The fix is a card update, or a link the member taps to re-enter it.
That happens in production, with the right role. Never from this demo.
Back out to the many-customer trail. In Sales, open the declined credit card log.
Seven columns lay out every failed charge:
date, customer, plan, amount, reason, attempts, location.
When a member swears they paid and your record says declined, reason and attempts settle it.
Search by customer or location to narrow it.
One caution from today's capture: the list came back empty with a fetch error.
Don't read that as no declines.
Confirm the data actually loaded before you tell a member their account is clean.
When it's the whole site, go to Settings, then Locations.
The locations list carries code, name, address, and last sync.
And in the side menu, notice Payment Credential Approval.
That's the gate every credential change has to pass through. Hold that thought.
Open the site and read its Payments and Credentials tab.
The processors sit in named blocks: Open Edge, Smart Tech, Other, and Card Connect.
You report which block is involved.
You never read the masked values, and you never hand-edit them.
Any change goes through Payment Credential Approval, not this screen.
Different complaint, different page.
If it's about text or email charges, open Billing, then Usage and Billing.
The Usage by Source panel breaks every charge down by campaign and automation:
source, type, volume, cost, share.
Card declines and messaging bills are two separate questions. This answers the second one.
Now build the ticket. Name the gateway.
Attach a few decline samples with their reason and attempt count.
Give the totals and the site or member affected.
And the hard line: no card numbers, no credentials, not even masked.
You diagnose and route. The retry and the fix happen upstream.
So that's the playbook.
Read the decline rate to size it. Scope to one member or many.
Follow it to Customer three-sixty, the declined log, or a site's gateway.
Check Billing when the charge was really about messaging.
Then escalate with the gateway, the samples, and the counts.
You touched nothing. You just found the truth and passed it on.
